Title: Safeguarding Traditional Palestinian Food Culture: The Case Of The Arab American Play Food And Fadwa
Object Description (Abstract): Turkish scholar Nesrin Yavaş writes about the safeguarding of Palestinian food culture as a "culinary sumud," a resilient practice of daily resistance against Israeli "culinary colonialism". Analyzing the 2012 Arab American play Food and Fadwa, she focuses on how traditional dishes and practices serve as a grassroots strategy to reclaim national identity, land, and sovereignty. The author further argues that the 2003 UNESCO Convention is ill-equipped to protect heritage in settler-colonial contexts because its framework overlooks the political complexities of displacement and contested claims to the soil. This 13-page work of critical analysis (medium difficulty) situated within Food, Heritage, and Literary Studies requires familiarity with the geopolitical history of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict for context.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Nationalism; food; cultural heritage; food traditions; sovereignty
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Food Studies, Cultural Heritage Studies, Nationalism Studies, and Literary/Drama Studies

Title: "We Palestinian Refugees"-Heritage as the Clothing of Bare Life: Reconfiguring Paradox, Obligation, and Imperative in Palestinian Refugee Camps in Jordan
Object Description (Abstract): UK scholar Beverley Butler and Jordanian researcher Fatima al-Nammari write about the performative use of "popular heritage rites" by Palestinian refugees in Jordan as a creative strategy to "clothe" the state of "bare life". Drawing on qualitative "heritage ethnographies" conducted across five refugee camps, they focus on how traditional material culture—specifically the embroidered thobe—serves as a "unique fingerprint" to reclaim national identity and human dignity. The authors further argue that these grassroots practices enable residents to navigate the "unresolved" paradox of "permanent impermanence" while resisting the dehumanizing "passive victim" labels imposed by humanitarian and state apparatuses. This 12-page work of critical ethnographic and archaeological analysis (high difficulty) situated within Heritage, Architecture, and Anthropology requires familiarity with the political philosophy of Giorgio Agamben and the history of the 1948 al-Nakba for context.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Thobe; al-Nakba; al-Naksa; heritage ethnography
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Contemporary Archaeology, Heritage Studies, Anthropology, and Architecture.

Title: Representations of Palestinian Culture in the Digital Public Sphere: A Semiotic Analysis of the Thobe and the Keffiyeh
Object Description (Abstract): Palestinian scholar Shadi Abu-Ayyash writes about the contemporary representation of national garments—the thobe and keffiyeh—in the digital public sphere as a contemporary arena for the practice of "cultural resistance". Drawing on a qualitative semiotic analysis of 40 tweets extracted from four major X (formerly Twitter) hashtag campaigns, he focuses on how these traditional symbols serve as "alternative cultural narratives" to challenge negative stereotypes and reclaim national heritage. The author further argues that these digital grassroots practices enable Palestinians and their global supporters to bypass biased Western mainstream media frames while resisting the "systematic policies of silencing" and Israeli cultural appropriation. This 14-page work of critical media analysis, situated within Media and Communication Studies, requires familiarity with Roland Barthes’s semiotic framework of denotation and connotation and the history of the 1948 Nakba for context.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: semiotic analysis; cultural resistance;  activism; traditional garments; digital narratives
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Media and Communication Studies

Title: Teachers at the crossroads: Culture, empathy, and emotional regulation in Palestinian society in Israel
Creator/Author: Kholoud Shanbour, Tsafrir Goldberg, Jenny Kurman
Academic Discipline: Education; Psychology; Learning and Instructional Sciences
Object Description (Abstract): Palestinian scholar Kholoud Shanbour and colleagues write about the intersection of cultural orientations, empathy, and emotion regulation among Palestinian Arab teachers in Israel as they navigate classroom management within a sociopolitical landscape marked by the tension between traditional collectivist values and modern pedagogical models. Drawing on an Epistemic Network Analysis (ENA) of semi-structured interviews with 30 educators from Arab public schools, they focus on how teachers utilize three distinct profiles—vertical collectivism, horizontal collectivism, and a dual approach—to negotiate the co-occurrence of authority, care, and emotional control. The authors further argue that these emotional processes are shaped by culturally mediated "feeling rules" and hierarchical structures, suggesting that culturally grounded professional development is essential for integrating empathetic responsiveness into disciplinary practices while resisting the emotional dissonance and burnout common in minority
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Arab-Palestinian teachers; Classroom management; Epistemic Network Analysis (ENA);
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Educational Psychology and Teacher Education.

Title: Decolonizing Cultural Heritage Theory: A Critical Reframing from the Palestinian Context
Creator/Author: Ziad M. Shehada; Mohammed H Alaqad
Academic Discipline: Critical Heritage Studies; Decolonial Theory; Digital Media Studies; Architecture
Object Description (Abstract): Ziad M. Shehada and Mohammed H. Alaqad write about the intersection of decolonial theory, community-led stewardship, and digital media activism among Palestinian communities as they navigate a sociopolitical landscape marked by the tension between Eurocentric conservation frameworks and the lived realities of colonial erasure. Drawing on a qualitative, critical-interpretive analysis of archival documents, oral histories, and social media content from platforms like Instagram and YouTube, they focus on how the proposed "Liberation Heritage" framework utilizes local knowledge—incorporating Waqf traditions, living rituals, and digital storytelling—to negotiate the co-occurrence of cultural survival, resistance, and identity preservation.The authors further argue that these heritage practices are shaped by the transformative power of digital technologies and communal memory, suggesting that a justice-oriented, community-led model of stewardship is essential for reclaiming cultural sovereignty while resisting the hegemonic "monumental authenticity" traditionally imposed by Western institutions. This 20-page work of Critical Heritage Studies, situated within Decolonial Theory and Digital Media Studies, requires familiarity with the Venice Charter and the Nara Document on Authenticity for context.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Liberation Heritage; Digital Media; Cultural Survival; Decolonial Theory
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Critical Heritage Studies and Decolonial Theory.

Title: Clashing Decolonial and Sociocultural Factors: Revisiting Palestinian Queer Liberation Using the Events of Summer 2019
Object Description (Abstract): Palestinian scholar Izat El Amoor writes about the internal ideological tensions within Palestinian queer activism following a series of transformative events in the summer of 2019 as a critical juncture for "queer liberation". Drawing on a qualitative analysis of 53 semi-structured interviews with queer Palestinian activists and non-activists, alongside media content analysis, he focuses on the "clash" between decolonial frameworks—which prioritize resisting Israeli pinkwashing—and sociocultural approaches that emphasize addressing internal societal homophobia. The author further argues that a strictly "radical purist" decolonial stance can be perceived as exclusionary or elitist, potentially alienating individuals whose lived experiences are primarily shaped by internal cultural, familial, and patriarchal structures. This 23-page work of qualitative sociological research, situated within Sociology and Anthropology, requires familiarity with concepts of "homonationalism" and "pinkwashing" as well as the intersectional complexities of the Israeli-Palestinian occupation for context.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: queer activism; homonationalism; decolonization; sociocultural factors; intersectionality
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Sociology and Anthropology within the context of Sexuality and Middle Eastern Studies

Title: Black Woman Radicals (BWR) Advocacy Manifesto: Intersectional Feminist Analysis of Afro-Palestinian Layered Oppression
Creator/Author: Arina Nihayati; Larasati Larasati; Zhillan Zhalila Fahlevi; Luwemba Musa Maswanku
Journal Title: Jurnal Ilmu Sosial dan Ilmu Politik (JISIP
Object Description (Abstract): This study analyzes how the Black Women Radicals (BWR) movement advocates for Afro-Palestinians through an intersectional and transnational feminist lens. The Afro-Palestinian community, residing primarily in Jerusalem’s African Quarter, experiences layered oppression as both Black individuals and Palestinians under Israeli military occupation. By employing an integrative review of manifestos and campaigns, the article illustrates how BWR’s initiatives—such as the School for Black Feminist Politics and the Black Women Radicals Database—amplify marginalized voices and document resistance. The findings highlight that BWR reframes Afro-Palestinian struggles as a global human rights concern, linking them to broader movements against settler colonialism, racism, and imperialism
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Afro-Palestinian; Intersectional Feminism; Settler Colonialism; Global Solidarity
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): International Relations, Political Science, and Gender Studies.

Title: Preserving Identity: How Palestinians Protect and Celebrate Their Heritage in the Face of Erasure
Journal Title: Washington Report on Middle East Affairs
Academic Discipline: Cultural Studies; Middle Eastern Studies; Political Science
Object Description (Abstract): This article examines the tenacious celebration of Palestinian identity through its most iconic cultural symbols: embroidery (tatreez), the keffiyeh, and cuisine. As these symbols face appropriation and the threat of erasure, they have been transformed into tools of resistance and solidarity both within Palestine and throughout the Diaspora. The author discusses how specific initiatives, such as the Palestine Heirloom Seed Library, work to maintain food sovereignty against restrictive policies, while designers and artists use traditional crafts to raise global awareness. Ultimately, the text asserts that the act of practicing and sharing these traditions is an essential defiance against oppression, ensuring that Palestinian culture remains a vibrant source of strength for future generations.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: tatreez; keffiyeh; cultural appropriation; resistance; Diaspora
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Cultural studies, political advocacy

Title: MUSLIM SOLIDARITY WITH PALESTINE WHILST LIVING ON STOLEN LAND: THE POLITICS OF RAMADAN IN AUSTRALIA
Object Description (Abstract): This paper contextualizes Muslim celebrations within a neoliberal understanding of politics and the consequent erasure of Indigenous identity. It traces the overlap of spectacles of Israeli brutality against Palestinians during Ramadan with official Ramadan and Eid celebrations in Australia, where the Islamic concept of "ummah" is invoked to mobilize solidarity. Using Foucauldian discourse analysis, the author analyzes the discursive repertoires of Muslim "representatives" in their engagements with the Australian settler-colonial state. The study outlines how respectability politics and liberal multicultural agendas pursued by leadership legitimate settler-colonial relations of power, demonstrating how this undermines a praxis of ummah as a potential call to decolonial, anti-imperial struggle on both stolen land in Australia and in solidarity with Palestinians.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Ramadan; Eid; Ummah; Respectability politics; Spatial politics
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Muslim Studies, Indigenous Studies

Title: The feasibility of investment in the religious heritage in anti-corruption efforts in Palestinian public organizations
Object Description (Abstract): This research addresses the feasibility of utilizing religious heritage as a tool for anti-corruption in Palestinian public organizations. Utilizing a mixed-method approach, the study combines a theoretical review of the relationship between religion and ethics with a quantitative survey of 800 public officials. The data reveal that while religious investment in current organizational culture is moderate, employees' anti-corruption attitudes are more strongly influenced by religious beliefs than by legal frameworks. Furthermore, employees expressed high future expectations (74.1%) for the success of these efforts. The study argues that integrating religious values into administrative policies is feasible and desirable as a complementary strategy to traditional legal controls. Recommendations include developing religious-based codes of conduct and specialized training to foster integrity and accountability.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Anti-corruption; Religious heritage; Public sector
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Public Administration, Law, and Sociology

Title: Geographies of the Body: Music, Violence and Manhood in Palestine
A community-based qualitative study of intergenerational resilience with Palestinian refugee families facing structural violence and historical trauma
Topic Location - Longitude: United Nations (UN) refugee camp in the West Bank near Jerusalem and Hebron region, otherwise unknown
Topic Location - Latitude: United Nations (UN) refugee camp in the West Bank near Jerusalem and Hebron region, otherwise unknown
Object Description (Abstract): Palestinian-American professor of psychology Devin G. Atallah writes about the transgenerational transmission of trauma and resilience within Palestinian displaced families in the mid-2010s, from generations that survived the Nakba to teenagers. The author, drawing on group interviews with community members in a UN refugee camp in the West Bank, finds three categories of resilience passed down through Palestinian families: resistance as a function of survival and protecting family, remembering their belonging in Palestinian history and culture, and practicing intercommunal and cultural mutual support. This academic paper is not unusually dense for a write-up of its kind but still requires a base level of post-1948 Palestinian history and high levels of reading comprehension skill.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: community-based research, family resilience, historical trauma, Palestinian refugees, qualitative methods, structural violence, transgenerational transmission
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): psychiatry; Palestine studies; mental health

Title: Heritage between Resistance and Government in Palestine
Journal Title: International Journal of Middle East Studies
Object Description (Abstract): Prof. Chiara de Cesari, who studies heritage and memory, considers in a concise essay that heritage and archaeology—which was and is a tool to subjugate Palestinian history—is also used to restore extensive areas of the West Bank, reviving cities destroyed by colonialism with urban regeneration, an effort that itself can be co-opted towards neoliberal capital accumulation or towards genuine freedom.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Heritage, NGOs, urban regeneration, restoration, archaeology, nationalist movements, democratic movements, cultural governmentality

Title: Visual Representation, Propaganda and Cyberspace: The Case of the Palestinian Islamist Movements
Academic Discipline: Visual studies; digital studies; Middle East studies
Object Description (Abstract): Prof. Attila Kovacs of comparative religion studies the relationship between modern Islamic interpretations of visual representations and media and Palestinian Islamist movements such as Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad. He argues that some modern interpretations of Islam have begun to reform their definitions of visual representations and its relationship to idolatry, which exists in line with the increasing use of visual media to represent and propagandize for Islamist Palestinian resistance groups.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: identity, social media, media studies, communication studies, websites, conflict, Internet studies
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Visual studies; Middle East studies; Internet studies; modern Islamic interpretation

Title: Spirits of Palestine: Gender, Society, and Stories of the Jinn
Object Description (Abstract): Celia Rothenberg, who studies anthropology and religion in Palestine, studies the Palestinian Muslim village of Artas in the West Bank, exploring daily life through the lens of possession by Islamic spirits as a communal and personal experience that can be fundamentally negative, positive or neutral, allowing Palestinians to gain spiritual and sometimes physical agency in the face of occupation.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: jinn, West Bank, Artas, spirituality, possession, ethnography, daily life, resistance
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Ethnography; religion & spirituality; gender studies

Title: Tweet Like It’s Free: Civic Imagination in the 2021 Palestinian Unity Intifada
Object Description (Abstract): Palestinian-American academic Prof. Sulafa Zidani discusses the #Tweet_Like_It’s_Free trend during the 2021 Unity Intifada, wherein Palestinian Twitter users would tweet from a world in which Palestine was liberated and Palestinian citizens could travel freely without the threat of violence. She argues that this method of hope is necessary in its function as a survival tactic and that Palestinians’ use of internet to propagate this trend allowed the message of hope to travel beyond local communities and throughout Palestine and the diaspora.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: civic imagination,digital media,creative insurgency,liberation,Twitter,qualitative analysis,Internet analysis,Palestinian media
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Internet studies; media analysis; Palestine studies

Title: Sumud and Food: Remembering Palestine Through Cuisine in Chile
Journal Title: Mashriq & Mahjar: Journal of Middle East and North African Migration Studies
Object Description (Abstract): Chilean-American professor Nicholas Bascuñan-Wiley conducts interviews with a variety of Palestinians living in Chile, using their cooking and associated memories as a starting point to discuss their cultural and diaspora identity and connection to their heritages. He argues that food culture across the Palestinian diaspora represents sumud—steadfastness in Arabic—and resistance from occupation.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Chile, Diaspora, Memory, Foodways, Palestinians in Chile, Palestinian diaspora, heritage
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Sensory ethnography; diaspora studies; transcultural studies

Title: Children of the “Idea of Palestine”: Negotiating Identity, Belonging and Home in the Palestinian Diaspora
Object Description (Abstract): Dr. Victoria Mason, based on interviews with Palestinians whose families were exiled first from Palestine following the Nakba and Six-Day War and again fled Kuwait due to the 1990-1991 Gulf War to emigrate to Australia, analyzes the experience of Palestinian and diaspora identity based on generational barriers. She argues that generations further from exile have increasingly dual identities in the country where they live and their homeland, which was complicated by anti-Arab and Muslim sentiments in their new home country.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Belonging; Diaspora; Exilic generations; Australian Palestinians; Gulf War
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Diaspora studies; intercultural studies; ethnography

Title: Being/longing: visualizing belonging with Palestinian refugee children
Object Description (Abstract): American Prof. David Marshall, in allowing children living in the West Bank to digitally narrate the story of their life in their refugee camps, analyzes the creation of identity of young refugees from Palestine. He finds that these children produce a sense of collective belonging through their communities and a shared desire to return to their colonized homes, and that the provision of creative agency via place-based digital storytelling allowed them to authentically share their stories.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Children; belonging; visual methods; digital storytelling; autoethnography; belonging, refugees; West Bank
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Political geography, digital storytelling, digital ethnography

Title: Postspatial, Postcolonial: Accessing Palestine in the Digital
Object Description (Abstract): University of California, Irvine professor Meryem Kamil analyzes two new media projects—one that provides 360-degree virtual tours of the al-Aqsa compound in heavily surveilled East Jerusalem and another that visualizes a destroyed Palestinian village—to conclude that similar projects can successfully work towards the decolonization of Palestine by making its land and culture accessible in the face of limitations to physical access of Palestine.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: New media; settler colonialism; journalism; media analysis; al-Aqsa; Internet; decolonialism
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Media studies; postcolonial studies; new media studies; heritage studies

Title: ‘Othering’ the archive—from exile to inclusion and heritage dignity: the case of Palestinian archival memory
Object Description (Abstract): UCL Professor Beverley Butler, in conversation with philosophers and authors discussing archival institutions, concludes that the Zionist movement as a dominant colonial archive imposes a literalization of memory onto the land of Palestine, whereas Palestinian archives are less formalized and based on fractured memories. She argues that it is important for the idea of archives to expand to archival memory and to support the preservation of Palestinian identity.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Cultural politics; archives; philosophy; cultural heritage; archival memory
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Archival science, cultural heritage

Title: Fragmented Space And Violence In Palenstine
Object Description (Abstract): American professor Anne Marie Baylouny examines how Osla-Era land fragmentation in the Palestinian West Bank reshaped political mobility and increased level of violence in Fragmented Space And Violence In Palenstine published in the International Journal on World Peace. Data came from historical record and political-violence events in Gaza, referencing multiple sources. In the article, Baylouny utilizes qualitative political anlysis that links changes in geography to collective action outcomes. The background knowledge needed is moderate where one needs to have basic knowledge of the Oslo process, but the article explains more conceptual and background knowledge in its introduction. The article is around 30 pages and very academic with medium difficulty to read.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Space; Mobility; Fragmentation
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Palestinian studies, Political history, geography

Title: The Health Dimensions of Violence in Palestine: A Call to Prevent Genocide
Object Description (Abstract): The article The Health Dimensions of Violence in Palestine: A Call to Prevent Genocide is written by a mix of authors from England, Australia, and American (some without public record). The authors directly call on all parties to ensure that all Palestinians can fully realise their right to life, liberty, and security of person as protected under international law and detailed in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights by stating the severity of the health situation in Palestine with the attack that aims to destory key infrastrucutre. The main source of data is public reporting and legal texts, where the author analyzes the public health report and calls for action. You need minimal background knowledge, and it is only 1 page with low diffciulty.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Health; Slow-motion genocide; Accessibility
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Health, infrastructure

Title: Forced or Development Induced Displacement? Occupied Palestinian Territories and International Conscience
Object Description (Abstract): Bangladeshi Ullah argues that displacement in occupied East Jerusalem is not incidental, but it is produced through annexation policies and projects framed to solidify the unification of Jerusalem. The article “Forced or Development Induced Displacement? Occupied Palestinian Territories and International Conscience” is published on JID which is an open-access source. Ullah utlizes secondary sources and documentary data that involves Plestine studies for his conceptual and qualitative analysis with case-based reasoning. You need a moderate level of background knowledge with basic familarity of human rights and international law to read this 13 page with medium difficulty since it is written in formal policy language.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Forced Displacement; Systematic Exclusion; Permits; Annexation
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Palestinian studies, Political history, geography, architecture

Title: The Relationship between Multiple Exposures to Violence and War Trauma, and Mental Health and Behavioural Problems among Palestinian Children and Adolescents
Object Description (Abstract): Palestinian author Basel El-Khodary and British author Muthanna Samara are professors of Islamic University of Gaa and Kignston University London. They designed this observational experiment where quantitative surveys and questionnaires, consisting of 25 items, were given to children in Gaza Strip from age of 11-17 across different genders and family sizes. They discovered that the child and adolescent suffered not only one type of violence but violence was exposed and took various forms in multiple settings - violence at home, in the neighbohood, and at school. The article addres mental health impact on Palestanian children and adolescents which could be hard to read since it is rigorously scientific but you need a low level of background knowledge where common understanding of PTSD and anxiety is the only necessary backgournd information needed.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Mental Health; Multiple Exposures; Children & Adolescents
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Child and Adolescent psychiatry; trauma studies

Title: Israeli and Palestinian Memories and Historical Narratives of the 1948 War—An Overview
Object Description (Abstract): Israeli authors Avraham Sela and Alon Kadish from Iraq and America wrote this 26 pages analysis on the collective memories of 1948 War, arguing that memory, both individual and collective, is elastic in nature, spontaneous, sentimental, and susceptible to psychological factors, lacking “scientific” or rational rules, and concerned with the public rather than the small community of professional historians. They also argue that the Oslo process increased the conflict over identiy and legitimacy. The article addres the Israeli- Jewish and Israeli- Palestinian memories, showing deep analysis on history and memory studies. Some background information abdou 1948 War is needed to read this article, it includes many conceptual vocabulary from history which makes it moderately difficult to read. JSTOR as a paid, subscription-based library is accesible with most institutions.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Nakba; Diversity; Collective memory; Remembrance
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): history, memory studies, anthropology

Title: Screaming, Silence and Mass Violence in Isarael/Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): The Dutch-Turkish author Ugur Umit Ungor argues in “Screaming, Silence and Mass Violence in Isarael/Palestine” that the current debate around genocide does not reach consensus, pulling scholars to different directions. The debate itself falls into seletive indignation and decontextualization, suggesting some voices are being amplified while others are muted. The article addresses how screaming and silence shape interpretation of mass violence. The author use public disclosed sources and interview Palestinina-Syrians to analyze the damaging pattern of the current disclosure. It is relative difficult to read this article without some previous knowledge on genocide definition debates and recent Isareal/Paestine conflict context, and most institutions provide access to this journal.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Silence; Genocide; Mass violence
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Genocide Studies, political history, war

Title: Violence as Strategy: The Palestinian Case
Object Description (Abstract): In “Violence as Strategy: The Palestinian Case”, American author James F. Miskel argues that the Palestinian strategy of fear-inducing violence has not been effective in terms of generating progress toward Palestinian goals and whether the strategy has been efficient in terms of its expenditure of Palestinian resources. The author suggests that political choice shapes the definiton of violence. Miskel collects qualitative eidnece, such as publicly known events and analyzes strategically through treating violence as a political goal. The First Intifada is one of the events that the author mentioned to illustrate how tactics could shape media and public sympathy. The reader needs to have a moderate level of knowledge on conflict and First Intifada to understand the article. This 10 pages long article is not open access from Duke University Press, but most educational institutions collaborate and share access to this article with each other.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Strategy; Media; First Intifada
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Political Science, cultural studies, Palestinaina studies

Title: The Defining Images of the Israel-Hamas War
Object Description (Abstract): This CNN photo-gallery gathers defining images taken in Palestine/ Israel in 2023, visually documenting the ongoing and widespread impact and human consequences of the conflict. Methodologically, it works as a visual narrative of the destruction, daily survival, displacement, and commemoration. This article includes striking pictures of children running for cover as bumbs fall, escaping into shelter, “trial of dead bodies” lying in the middle of the road, and death of infants and children. This article requires a low level of background knowledge since the images are captioned by the scenarios and background information. It is around ten pages long that is easily accessible on CNN.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Destruction; Children & Adolescents; Images
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Journalism, Photography, architecture

Title: Humiliation: the invisible trauma of war for Palestinian Youth
Creator/Author: Rita Giacaman, Niveen M.E. Abu-Rmeileh, etc
Object Description (Abstract): In “Humiliation: the invisible trauma of war for Palestinian Youth”, the Palestianian researchers Rita Giacaman, Niveen M.E. Abu-Rmeileh, and etc conducted a survey, sampling from 3415 students of the Rmallah District. The results demonstrate that exposure to violence and trauma is significantly associated with SHC in the Occupied Palestinian Territory. There are discussions about how humiliation could greatly affect health for both boys and girls in a distinct way by a direct positive correlation with the SHC. The researchers argue that the humiliation should be included as an indictator and predictor in health research in post-war or conflict contexts. You need some level of basic statistical and health-realted background knowledge to fully understand the research. This article is around 12 pages and is accessible online but subscription is needed, or some institution will provide the access.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Humiliation; Health outcomes; Children & Adolescents
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Public health, Scientific Research, Stastitical research

Title: Immigration and Labor in Palestin
Object Description (Abstract): The article “Immigration and Labor in Palestine” by author Andrew McFadyean focuses on the analysis of the immigration and labor policy in British Mandate Palestine. The author aruges that stability required economic integration and reduced barriers for employment in Jewish enterprise through qualititave and observational data by quoting and referencing other articles. McFadyean states that the immigration is highly restricted because of the lack of labor and rising wages that contribute to economic slowdown and instability. In order to understand this article, you need background knowledge on policy, immigration and economics concepts. JSTOR as a paid, subscription-based library is accesible by most institutions.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Immigration; Restriction; Economics
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Foreign Affair, political studies, Palestinain sudies, political history; social science

Title: The Palestinian Nakba and its Continuous Repercussions
Object Description (Abstract): This journal article by Adel Manna, a Palestinian historian, argues that the Nakba should not only be seen as a single war, but as a long-term, ongoing event that causes impacts that continuously shape Palestinians from the past to present. While early accounts emphasize the political outcome of the 1948 war and marginalizes the sufferings the Palestinians are going to face, with the support of historical records and academic works, Manna traces back the cause of the war to decades ago and highlights the structural transformations of Palestine. Though Palestine reconstituted a new identity after they experienced statelessness, displacement, and loss of homeland right after, the catastrophe continued to deepen as subsequent events, such as the 1967 occupation, lead to permanent occupation and colonization. Manna frames displacement as an ongoing historical condition that goes beyond one war and one displacement, demonstrating the consequences in the long term. It also offers many examples of displacement, showing its different form throughout a long historical time span, highlighting how displacement has not stopped. This article also gives a new perspective of how historical events are interpreted and reinterpreted over time. It is a moderate difficulty journal article that requires some background context regarding Israeli-Palestinian, but still readable if not.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Nakba; Palestine Identity; Fragmentation
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Palestinian studies, Political history, Modern middle east history

Title: Necropolitical Debris: The Dichotomy of Life and Death
Object Description (Abstract): This journal article by Nadera Shalhoub-Kevorkian, a Palestinian scholar and professor in law and criminology, argues that the Nakba is both a historical event at 1948 but also an ongoing settler colonial condition in the form of legal dispossession, identity control, and narrative erasure. This is a short to medium length article with moderate difficulty but requires some knowledge regarding the 1948 war and familiarity with legal and political vocabulary. In particular, the author focused on the Nakba Bill that penalizes institutions that commemorate Independence Day as a day of mourning and reject Israel as a Jewish state, emphasizing how Israel has enacted a law that attempts to deny the Nakba, silence trauma, discipline Palestinian identity, inflicting violence psychologically and emotionally. The author then reveals a deeper claim about the dichotomy of life and death, where the birth of Israel depended on the death of Palestine and Palestinian memory. The author introduces how physical displacement can be shifted to narrative and legal displacement and how violence can also be psychological and emotional, not only in the form of military. While the article introduces new perspectives on the form of displacement and violence, it highlights how law creates territorial displacement and psychological erasure and brings in thoughts on how narratives can be easily suppressed and often marginalized.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Nakba; Forced displacement; Psychological violence
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Criminology, Human rights studies, Political history

Title: The Palestinians in Lebanon: Social Repercussions of Israel's Invasion
Object Description (Abstract): This journal article by a Palestinian-American historian Rashid Khalidi examines multiple facets of the consequences of the 1982 Israeli invasion of Lebanon for Palestinian refugees. This short to medium length journal article contains dense political analysis that requires certain background knowledge on the PLO and the Lebanese Civil War. The author introduces that, during the period of Israeli invasion, the displaced Palestinians had entered the rare state of autonomy, where self-governance and strong economic presence were the characteristics. However, Israel’s invasion, mainly targeting the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO), caused territorial and structural fragmentation. It further creates negative social effects, the loss of political agency, long-term insecurity, and uncertainty of Palestine’s future. Khalidi discusses Israel’s main responsibility in this event due to their initiation and the US’s responsibility as they provided military and diplomatic support for Israel. While this new vulnerability and displacement was caused by Israel’s invasion, the author also points out the pre-existing vulnerability of the Palestinians due to the lack of citizenship. This article reveals the psychological, social, and political consequences of violence and displacement and highlights the cycling cause and effect of war and displacement. Khalidi also goes beyond the immediate effect by examining how it dismantles social structures continuously and how violence and conflict is integrated into complex global power systems.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Israeli invasion 1982; Refugee; Autonomy
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): History; Political science

Title: Intermediality and Cultural Journalism
Object Description (Abstract): This journal article by Ahmed Morsi and Hala Halim argues that the 1967 Naska is being actively interpreted and reshaped by journalism and literature. Starting with the introduction of Ahmed Morsi, an Egyptian cultural journalist and artist, the authors expand on the cultural journalism in the Arab world and focus on the reaction of Egyptian journalism on the 1967 Naska. The Naska caused a crisis in nationalist ideology, causing the Egyptians, who controlled Gaza, to start an inward cultural critique and self-critique. While Palestinians were not the primary actors in the war, the consequences of the conflict centered on them through occupation and renewed displacement. In this time period, journalism became a way where emotions and trauma were being processed and presented to the public. This article highlights the role the media plays in constructing collective memory and processing catastrophe and violence. Though this article does not directly focus on displacement and violence, it centers on an event deeply related to the two concepts and views these two concepts through the lens of media, reflecting how media affect catastrophes and how catastrophes influence media. The article brings in a new perspective that violence is not only physical but also discursive, and that displacement is not merely geographic but destabilizes social, ideological, and cultural frameworks.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Naksa; Journalism; Media
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Memory studies; Political analysis; History

Title: The Banality of Brutality: British Armed Forces and the Repression of the Arab Revolt in Palestine, 1936-39
Object Description (Abstract): This article written by Matthew Hughes analyzes the British military repression on Palestinians during the period of the Arab Revolt (1936-1939) and argues that the colonial brutality was incorporated in their governance structure. With the support of evidence from British military archives and records, the author demonstrates how the British have executed collective punishments, demolitions, evacuations, and destruction of homes as common measures. The article reveals how such colonial repression has gone beyond civil law, but became coercive control methods that weakened the Palestinians. At the same time, the article proves that violence started before 1949, rooting from years of social destabilization and mistreatment. This source highlights how the British military uses violence and displacement to reshape the Palestinian society, making them more fragmented and vulnerable. Even though the displacements are not massive exiles, they are internal dislocations. British forces shattered and killed Palestinian political leadership and weakened the village, making the Palestinian society both militarily and politically damaged before the 1948 war, creating more vulnerability for later mass displacement. This source establishes an early event regarding the Palestinians, noting the long-term consequence on Palestine. This source has a medium to long length and requires prior knowledge regarding the British occupation and their mandate system for the understanding of this article.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Arab Revolt; British Repression; Policy
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Colonial studies; military history

Title: The Battle of Karameh: A Landmark in the Palestine–Israel Conflict
Journal Title: The Indian Journal of Political Science
Object Description (Abstract): This short to medium length peer reviewed article by Subhash Singh examines the Battle of Karameh, claiming that this battle is a turning point where Palestinians begin to be seen as active and independent political agents who can resist and shape their own future. Through historical accounts, Singh recognized the root cause of this battle is the long-term displacement the Palestinians have experienced and the failure of the Arab military to make their promise in liberation unreachable. Background knowledge in events prior to this such as the 1948 and the 1967 Naksa helps understanding. Palestinians wanted to show that they could fight without the dependence on Arab states, launching cross-border attacks on Israel. The Palestinians survived while Israel withdrew their forces, completing a successful resistance act and shifting Palestinian identity. This article offers a comprehensive view of the battle while highlighting the impact of this event on the identity of Palestinians. At the same time, it provides an insight on the relationship between violence and displacement, as they form a cyclic causal relationship due to how violence further causes displacement and how displacement further reproduces violence. It also introduces the complication of violence as it shows that sometimes, unfortunately, violence may be the loudest way to resist for some groups of people, though it causes irreparable suffering as well. The implication about displacement and violence makes this piece fits well in the displacement and violence subtopic of the collection. Its narrative on the Palestinians resistance that shifted their position centers on morality.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Battle of Karameh; Resistance; Palestinian identity
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Arab Revolt; Collective punishment; British; Colonial policy;

Title: The Jordanian–Palestinian Civil War of 1970: A Quest for Justice and Peace
Object Description (Abstract): This scholar journal article by Ezzat N. Slaieh presents the Jordanian–Palestinian Civil War in 1970 and positions it as a domestic struggle in Jordan that is shaped by long term unresolved displacement experienced by the Palestinians. Slaieh emphasizes the injustice that has influenced the Palestinians to reclaim agency, causing this internal crisis where Palestinian groups challenged the sovereignty of King Hussein, the king of Jordan, and where refugee camps grew to become sites of political radicalization. However, even after the war and the agreement to cease fire, instability maintained within Jordan and the Palestinian bitterness deepened. Slaieh claims that not until the problem of refugee and Palestinian rights issues are addressed, this type of struggle will persist. The war was not a solution to the problem, but a side effect of displacement that would continue to exist. This source presents the war in a regional perspective. This article expands violence and displacement beyond the occurrence when they happened, noting their impact to reshape the stability of the country and influence the people. This source presents another example of violence and displacement, further extending to how displacement is not durable and its effect to surrounding regions. This moderate difficult source also encourages critical thinking about sovereignty, refugee rights, and the political consequences of unresolved decisions, though it may require some knowledge in previous Israeli-Palestine wars.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Jordanian–Palestinian Civil War; Resistance; Refugee
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Political science; foreign policy studies

Title: The Arab-Palestine, Israel Conflict, 53 Years On: Perceptions and an Assessment
Object Description (Abstract): This scholar journal article by Robert Olson analyzes how different perspectives and influences from academic, cultural, political, and social aspects have shaped the development of the conflict involving Palestine, Israel, and Arab for decades. Robert divided his discussion into three parts, where he explains how academic bias exists while teaching due to politically sensitive information, how events like the holocaust and other cultural reinforced sympathy for Israel, and how the conflict is positioned within the British and American imperial strategy. The author explained how assisting Israel during the conflict between Israel and Palestine did not purely come out of strategic alliance, but was deeply influenced by the three perspectives. Beyond the Israel–Palestine context, the author provides general thought on how morality affects public perception and political policies. This source does not solely focus on an Israel or Palestine perspective, offering a unique perception of the ongoing conflict between Palestine and Israel. This article is also because it makes people think about the different factors that affect narrative and how narratives are constructed. This medium length piece focuses on an international view of the events of the Nakba, the 1967 occupation, and the Jordanian civil war, all of which are connected to violence and displacement, thus requiring certain knowledge on these incidents. This source strengthens the inclusion of the PPN site where it connects local displacement and violence to international response and power structures.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Arab-Israeli conflict; Media; The Holocaust; Policy
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Political science; foreign policy studies; media studies

Title: The Revolt of 1936: A Revision
Object Description (Abstract): In this peer-reviewed journal written by Matthew Kraig Kelly, he challenges the dominant narrative of the Revolt of 1936, reframing the revolt not only as an unrest but as a political uprising against British rule and Zionist settlement. In this period, under the rule of the British, Kelly challenges the idea that the British was harsher only in response to the revolt, claiming that the British, who started suppressive and punitive measures early on, intensified the revolt. With documents and military reports, Kelly critiques how the government’s own role in causing the revolt and making the revolt seem criminal is erased by dominant British accounts and believed by early historians. This source provides another unique perspective to past historical events, revealing how power structures impact narratives, where, in this case, the revolt as a response to repression is conceived as a criminal unrest. This article situates violence within the discussion of biased storytelling and the politics of historiography, encouraging thought on how narratives are being told. It brings up critical thinking regarding how displacement, repression, and narrative construction interact. This source is dense with archival evidence, and it requires certain knowledge in the British Mandate in Palestine.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: British Repression; Arab Revolt; Resistance
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): History, Middle Eastern studies, colonial history

Title: Blood and Tears in the Mirror of Memory: Palestinian Trauma in Liana Badr’s The Eye of the Mirror
Object Description (Abstract): This medium length article by Marie-Luise Kohlke explores Liana Badr’s novel The Eye of the Mirror, a book that depicts the Siege and massacre of the Palestinian refugee camp Tal el-Zaatar, a camp that represents the second time of displacement displacement for Palestinians. Due to the content of the book, this source requires knowledge in previous wars and the Lebanese Civil War. Through close textual analysis, Kohlke argues that this book recenters women in this period of history and that it focuses on trauma and witnessing. Kohlke also points out that it is hard for survivors to speak after experiencing traumatic events, showing how trauma and memory affects narrative. Gender influences narratives too, as Kohlke and Badr challenge the masculinist history perspective. This article discusses a book that situates the Siege and Tal el-Zaatar in the broader ideas of memory and gender while also considering concerns of paralleling this experience to the Holocaust and portraying how trauma is experienced, remembered, and creates influence across generations. Within the displacement and violence part of the collection, it shows how displacement and violence are also psychological and cultural as gendered experiences and survival testimony are being touched upon. This source demonstrates how voices are impacted and how they become narratives, encouraging students to think how history is told and whose suffering is recognized.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Trauma; The Siege; Tal el-Zaatar; Palestine; Liana Badr
Associated Pedagogical Cluster(s): Feminist literary criticism, trauma studies, memory studies

Title: Love Is a Revolutionary Emotion: Ingroup Solidarity amid Political Violence in Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): The article by Crystal Shackleford examines the relationship between political repression and solidarity. Through interviews with 386 West Bank Palestinians, she argues that political and military violence (PMV) often elicit stronger feelings of solidarity and collective commitment by strengthening ingroup love (not reinforcing outgroup hate). This finding contradicts traditional deterrence-based assumptions that assume stronger repression leads to less resistance. The study sits between social and political psychology and examines the driving emotional force behind resistance. Therefore, it requires some knowledge of psychology and statistics, although the abstract and conclusions are easily legible by most people.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Ingroup/Outgroup; Love/Hate; Sacred values; Survey; Psychology

Title: Statement of Scholars in Holocaust and Genocide Studies on Mass Violence in Israel and Palestine since 7 October
Object Description (Abstract): This statement gathers over 55 scholars in Holocaust and Genocide Studies to assess mass violence following 7 October 2023. It condemns atrocities committed by Hamas and the subsequent Israeli military response in Gaza. It highlights systematic civilian targeting, displacement, and destruction. The statement contextualizes these events within Israeli settler colonialism, occupation, and exclusionary nationalism, warning of genocide risk. It draws on publicly available reports, leader statements, and historical precedents, calling for urgent international action to protect civilians. Is it an easy read but requires some background knowledge of the October 7 attacks and the subsequent violence committed by Israel.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Mass Violence; Settler Colonialism; International Action

Title: The Psychological Toll of War and Forced Displacement in Gaza: A Study on Anxiety, PTSD, and Depression
Object Description (Abstract): This peer-reviewed article examines the mental health impacts of ongoing war and violence on Palestinians in Gaza, focusing especially on levels of trauma, anxiety, and depression among civilians living under repeated military attacks and displacement. They found high levels of anxiety, depression, and PTSD by examining the survey responses of 952 displaced adults as well as high levels of comorbidities. This is a medium-length, fairly difficult research article in psychology and mental health that requires some background knowledge of trauma studies and the broader context of the Gaza conflict.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Mental Health; PTSD; Anxiety; Depression; Survey

Title: The Grammar of Israeli Settler Colonial Violence and the Genocidal War Waged on Gaza: From the Nakba to the 7th of October 2023
Object Description (Abstract): This scholarly article examines how narratives about Gaza during wartime shape political understanding of violence, survival, and civilian suffering. Published in the sociopolitical journal Partecipazione e Conflitto, the author uses qualitative discourse analysis to explore how media, academic, and public narratives frame Gazan experiences after October 7, 2023. The article argues that storytelling about war is inherently political: it can either humanize civilians or reduce them to abstract symbols within geopolitical debates. Drawing on narrative interpretation and conflict discourse analysis, the piece shows how language influences global perceptions of Gaza and reinforces broader power dynamics in representations of war. This is a relatively dense academic article that requires some prior knowledge of political sociology and the Israel–Palestine conflict to fully understand.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Settler Colonialism; Linguistics; Speech; Dehumanization

Title: From laughter to survival: The effect of war on children’s play in Gaza
Creator/Author: Dana Bdier; Bilal Hamamra; Fayez Mahamid
Object Description (Abstract): This study examines how the Gaza war has altered children's play patterns. Researchers interviewed 30 individuals in Gaza between November 2024 and January 2025. They found that the conflict has stripped play of its joy, increasingly linking it to survival pressures. They found that children's play has become more violent and centered on daily survival struggles, with many children losing interest in play altogether. At the same time, play can sometimes help children cope with their emotions. The researchers noted that while war destroys childhood and well-being, supporting safe play and community mutual aid initiatives can help protect children's development and emotional health. While this doesn’t require much prior knowledge, the vocabulary makes this a medium-level read suitable for at least intermediate English speakers.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Mental Health; Children & Adolescents; Trauma; Coping Mechanisms

Title: War-related trauma in narratives of Gazans: challenges, difficulties and survival coping
Creator/Author: Bilal Hamamra; Fayez Mahamid; Dana Bdier; Mai Atiya
Object Description (Abstract): This paper examines how the war has impacted the mental health of Gaza Strip residents since the significant escalation of violence on October 7, 2023. Researchers interviewed 30 adult residents of Gaza, listening to their firsthand accounts of fear, loss, stress, and daily hardships. Through analyzing the interview content, researchers identified five core themes: “(1) challenges and difficulties faced Gazans, (2) traumatic experiences related to war, (3) feelings and emotions among Gazans living in refugee camps, (4) how do Gazans perceive the future and (5) survival coping employed by Gazans following the on-going genocide against the Gaza Strip”. The study reveals that trauma in the Gaza Strip is deeply entrenched and pervasive, intertwined with the past and present. The authors emphasize the urgent need for psychological support and humanitarian resources to assist the population in rebuilding their lives. The study requires some background knowledge of the Israel-Palestinian conflict, and the overall reading is medium to hard.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Trauma; Coping Mechanisms; Clinical Psychology

Title: Toward Nakba as a Legal Concept
Object Description (Abstract): In this article, law scholar Rabea Eghbariah argues that international law doesn’t have a good concept for describing the full experience of Palestinians. Most legal terms people use, like occupation, apartheid, or genocide, show only part the Palestinian experience. But none describe the long and ongoing process that started in 1948, when hundreds of thousands of Palestinians were displaced from their homes. Eghbariah argues that Nakba isn’t just a historical event; instead, it has become a structure of domination and legal fragmentation that keeps Palestinians in different legal categories (citizens, residents, refugees) with unequal rights. He proposes treating “Nakba” as its own legal concept because it has its own foundational violence (displacement), structure (fragmentation), and purpose (denial of self‑determination). By defining Nakba this way, the article hopes to help scholars and legal systems approach Palestine in a new right and stop relying on analogies and metaphors for Palestinian suffering. While the full text is dense and contains many legal concepts, the introduction is easy to understand for non-law persons, albeit requiring a bit of prior knowledge of Nakba and the Palestinian situation.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Nakba; International Law; Settler Colonialism; Legal Theory

Title: Anatomy of a Genocide
Language: English, Arabic, French, Spanish, Mandarin, Russian
Journal Title: Report to the UN Human Rights Council
Academic Discipline: Human Rights Law; International Law; Genocide Studies
Object Description (Abstract): This UN Human Rights Council report reviews the human rights situation in the occupied Palestinian territories, especially Gaza, after the major escalation of violence that began on 7 October 2023. The Special Rapporteur, Francesca Albanese, condemns attacks by armed Palestinian groups but focuses on the violence and policies carried out by Israel. The report describes mass killing, large‑scale displacement, heavy damage to homes and cities, and widespread trauma among civilians, evidenced by multiple independent sources. Based on the scale and nature of these actions, the report argues there are reasonable grounds to believe that Israel’s conduct may meet the legal threshold for genocide. It is a hard read because it is a UN report with its special formatting and structure requirements, and it also requires some prior knowledge of the Israel-Palestinian conflict.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Genocide; Human Rights Council; United Nations; International Law

Title: Israel’s Apartheid Against Palestinians: Cruel System of Domination and Crime Against Humanity
Object Description (Abstract): This comprehensive report released by Amnesty International provides an in-depth analysis of the laws, policies, and practices affecting Palestinians within Israel and Palestine. The organization highlights how multiple legal and political mechanisms, including land control, movement restrictions, deprivation of property rights, and unequal access to public services, collectively constitute a system of oppression and discrimination faced by Palestinians in their daily lives. Based on years of research and documented cases, the report concludes that this system meets the international legal definition of apartheid. It demands Israel abolish these practices and calls on nations and international institutions to pressure Israel to guarantee Palestinian human rights. While the report is lengthy and somewhat complex, requiring readers to have a basic understanding of the conflict's background and human rights law, its core arguments are sufficiently clear for high school and college students.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Apartheid; Human Rights; International Law; Settler Colonialism

Title: Voices from Gaza: Coping Strategies during the War on the Gaza Strip – A Qualitative Study
Object Description (Abstract): This article examines how people in Gaza cope psychologically while living through ongoing war and destruction. Using qualitative narratives and lived testimonies, the author focuses on everyday survival strategies such as emotional endurance, social support, and attempts to maintain normal life under extreme stress. Rather than portraying Gazans as traumatized victims, the study highlights resilience and adaptive coping in conditions of displacement, fear, and loss. The article is moderate in length and difficulty, and it requires some background knowledge about the 2023–present war on Gaza and the mental health impacts of prolonged conflict to fully understand its arguments.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Trauma; Coping Mechanisms; Mental Health

Title: Struggling for Time: Environmental Governance and Agrarian Resistance in Israel/Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): In this book, Gutkowski, a socio-cultural anthropologist working in the agrarian environment in Israel/Palestine, critically explores the ways in which time is leveraged as a mechanism of control by the Israeli state, as well as a site of resistance for Palestinian agricultural experts & workers. Specifically, Gutkowski argues that Israeli scientific & legal bureaucracy enacts environmental racism by erasing native narratives & perpetuating its own societal time by articulating Palestinian agriculture as biblical while also enacting the “capitalist time” of Israel’s industrialized agriculture time. In order to unpack the power structures that Gutkowski argues illustrate how settler society lays moral claim on native time, Gutkowski leverages ethnographic fieldwork in the form of interviews with Jewish-Israeli & Palestinian citizens, state officials, agronomists, planners, environmentalists & agriculturalists by traveling across policymaking arenas and agrarian sites in Israel. While background knowledge of settler-colonialism relationships, critical agrarian studies, & environmental anthropology would be helpful prior to reading this text, it is not necessary.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: agrarian governance; political ecology; agricultural temporality; environmental power

Title: Dwelling on the Green Line: Privatize and Rule in Israel/Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): In this book, Schwake illustrates the history of territorial/political dynamics in Israel/Palestine through the framework of the privatization of the housing market in lieu of traditional lenses such as statehood or military conflict. Specifically, Schwake argues that this privatization is, and has been, a key mechanism in rendering the West Bank’s territorial occupation into an annexation of Palestine as an Israeli habitat. Schwake supports this argument by means of connecting Israeli settlements on both sides of the border of the Green Line through analyzing architectural planning documents of suburban settlements, with a specific focus on middle-class housing along the Trans-Israel highway. While background knowledge of urban studies or Middle East politics would be helpful, it is not essential for understanding.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Housing privatization; urban planning; settlement development

Title: Static Displacement, Adaptive Domesticity: The Three Temporary Geographies of Firing Zone 918, Palestine
Journal Title: Making Home(s) in Displacement: Critical Reflections on a Spatial Practice
Object Description (Abstract): In this chapter of a larger book(Making Home(s) in Displacement: Critical Reflections on a Spatial Practice), Butmeh explores the concept of static displacement, where communities are not physically altered but are temporally or spatially constrained, through a case study of Masafer Yatta. Specifically, Butmeh makes the argument that the Israeli military designation of Firing Zone, while not forcing physical removal, has reshaped Palestinian life, and how residents have adapted through practices such as camouflage construction, nighttime agriculture, & collective grazing. Butmeh uses interviews with residents of Masafer Yatta as well as field surveys & legal planning documents to support the argument that everyday spatial practices can serve as forms of resistance within militarized planning regimes. While familiarity with critical geography, spatial theory, or settler-colonial studies would be helpful, it is not essential for understanding.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Static displacement; Masafer Yatta; Militarized planning; Spatial resistance

Title: On Ruins and Emptiness: Colonial Spatiality and Urban Politics in Palestine
Journal Title: City Re-construction: Urban Policy Innovation Towards Sustainable Cities in MENA Region
Academic Discipline: Human Geography, Architecture, Anthropology, Urban Studies
Object Description (Abstract): In this chapter, Bayarri draws on the work of theorists such as Walter Benjamin, Lefebvre, Fanon, & Gramsci to argue how demolition & policing/symbolic control(such as language of public signage) serve to normalize dispossession by aestheticizing destruction and characterizing Palestinian space as ‘absent’. Bayarri illustrates this by analyzing 4 urban-political scenarios/sites(Jaffa/Salama mosque site; Haifa protests and abandoned neighborhoods; Bab al-Shams on E1 land; Akka/Acre Arabic signage dispute) with a critical/decolonial geography lens, explaining how each function as political techniques rather than passive or neutral historical residues. While knowledge of basic Palestine/Israel history is helpful for understanding the material, it is not necessary.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Critical geography; Urban Politics; Ruins; Remnants

Title: Foragers
Academic Discipline: Film Studies, Visual Anthropology, Human Geography
Object Description (Abstract): In this film, Palestinian director & producer Jumana Manna argues through ethnographic observation and legal-cultural analysis that environmental protection frameworks & laws that regulate wild plant harvesting, while forms of ecological policy, also serve as a form of spatial governance/control over Palestinian land use. This idea is illustrated in this experimental documentary by mixing fiction, archival footage, & documentary of harvesting practices in Galilee, Golan Heights, & Jerusalem, winning the Camden International Film Festival for Special Mention for Best Documentary Feature and the Dokufest International Documentary and Short Film Festival for Green Dox 2022. While knowledge of basic Palestine/Israel history would be helpful, it is not necessary.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Foraging; environmental law; land governance

Title: Making Occupation & Energy Apartheid Bankable: How European Union Green Hydrogen Standards Reproduce Sovereignty Theft in Palestine
Academic Discipline: Energy Policy, Political Geography, Environmental Governance
Object Description (Abstract): This article is about how European Union green hydrogen and renewable fuel standards operate in the occupied Palestinian territories & specifically argues that environmental regulations shape questions of sovereignty and land & resource control. Specifically, Almassry compares two proposed Palestinian hydrogen projects to similar projects in bordering countries(Morocco, Jordan, Egypt) to demonstrate the main idea that viable developments in Palestine should be designed in a way that accommodates the realities of Israeli occupation rather than challenging them(for example, offshore arbitration or international trustees). Almassry expands the idea of architecture and environment beyond physical buildings to include energy infrastructure & resource governance, and highlights how environmental regulatory policy and infrastructural planning function as forms of spatial decision making that influence who has authority over land/water/economic development. Background knowledge of basic Palestine/Israel history & familiarity with energy policy is useful.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: energy infrastructure; sovereignty; environmental governance; occupation; climate justice

Title: Toward Sustainable Land Use of Urban Spaces in the Dense Areas: The Role of Organizing Activities in Enhancing the Flexible Environment in Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): This article speaks on moving towards practices of sustainable land use in urban areas, and specifically focuses on Nablus Old City in Palestine as a case study to explore how traditional public urban spaces in historic Palestinian city centers can support sustainable development in densely populated areas. In order to perform this analysis of Nablus Old City, Itma analyzes architectural features(such as enclosure, circulation patterns, & rectangular spatial design) along with a user survey measuring how varying age groups experience the space differently. Overall, the study’s main argument is the importance of enhancing flexibility, and that flexible design and diverse activity organization are what allows these historic spaces to stay relevant across generations, directly addressing traditional architecture/public space design/sustainability within a Palestinian context. Undergraduate level understanding of architecture and urban planning is helpful.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: urban space; sustainability; public squares; flexible enclosures

Title: Planting Palestine: Food, Environment, and the Arts of Solidarity
Academic Discipline: human geography; environmental humanities; cultural studies; Middle East studies
Object Description (Abstract): In this article, Boast highlights a recent environmental shift in contemporary Palestinian art and activism, specifically arguing that food/environmental-based narratives have emerged as very powerful tools for expressing identity and even building solidarity. The paper focuses on Jumana Manna’s documentary Foragers (2022) & explores how the criminalization of traditional foraging practices such as gathering za’atar and ‘akkoub is framed through broader environmental discourses like indigeneity/agroecology & food sovereignty. The author also notes that while this environmental framing can mobilize new audiences and political support, it can also risk, firstly, perpetuating the oversimplification of identity or, secondly, overlooking urban Palestinian life. Boast expands the idea of environment beyond physical land use or infrastructure to include cultural and artistic representations of land, food, and the overall idea of belonging.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: food sovereignty; environmental art; cultural activism; foraging practices; agroecology

Title: Settler Colonialism and the Environment in Al-Walaja, Palestine
Journal Title: The International Journal Of Human Rights
Academic Discipline: political geography; environmental studies; Middle East studies
Object Description (Abstract): In this article, Pearce analyzes how settler colonial practices influence environmental change in Al-Walaja, with a specific focus on land loss/agricultural decline. The article's main argument is that environmental policies(that are often framed as “green” initiatives) can function as tools of dispossession by masking land appropriation & normalizing territorial control, and oftentimes can serve as a form of greenwashing rather than genuinely benefiting the environment. The paper also explores how limits on construction and farming reshape social identity in addition to the physical landscape - which in turn, reinforce narratives that justify the settlement expansion that erases native agricultural traditions, directly connecting land use and political power & presenting a clear example of how environmental policy and spatial control operate together. Familiarity with middle east studies & human geography helpful but not required.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: greenwashing; environmental governance; land dispossession; agricultural decline;

Title: Predatory Fleas, Sterile Flies, and the Settlers: Agricultural Infrastructure and the Challenge of Alien-Native Dichotomies in Israel/Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): In this article, Shani draws on a four-year ethnographic study in the Arava/Arabah desert region to examine how agricultural infrastructure(such as wells, irrigation pipes, and even insects used for pest control!) shape both ecological systems and political relationships in Israel/Palestine. The article’s main argument is that infrastructure is not only technical or environmental but also political, as it creates hierarchies between communities and influences how land and resources are defined and controlled & also blurs boundaries between human and non-human actors. I selected this source because it expands the idea of architecture and environment beyond buildings to include agricultural systems, and allows us to consider landscapes that are actively engineered rather than simply only ones that are naturally occurring. Knowledge of environmental studies is helpful.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Pest control; environmental engineering; desert agriculture; territorial governance

Title: Slow Urbicide: A New Materialist Account of Political Violence in Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): This book examines the gradual destruction of Palestinian urban environments through the philosophical lens of materialism and theories of slow violence. It argues that urbicide in Israel/Palestine is carried out (in one way) through sustained spatial containment and the resulting reconfiguration of public memory. The author is an assistant professor of American Studies and Mass Media at University of Lodz in Poland. Background knowledge of Israel/Palestine and urban conflict is helpful but not required. 164 pages; upper-level undergraduate/post-graduate reading level.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Occupied territories; Urbicide; Geopolitics; Ethno-territoriality; Materialism; Political Violence

Title: Architecture after Revolution
Creator/Author: Sandi Hilal; Alessandro Petti; Eyal Weizman
Object Description (Abstract): This book explores, through theoretical architecture projects, the decolonization of architecture in Palestine. Using these projects by the DAAR (Decolonizing Architecture Art Research) collective in combination with photos and prior research, the authors propose design as a political method to provoke new forms of spatial thinking. It argues that reconceptualizing ownership and urbanism is central to decolonization. Historical knowledge on the Israel Palestine Conflict is needed. The authors, founders of DAAR, are architects from Palestine, Italy, and Israel. 206 pages; high school reading level.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: city planning; decolonization; 2000s; architecture; The Naksa; The 6 Day War; 1960s; Great Revolt; 1930s

Title: A Spatial Vision for Palestine: A Long-Term Plan That Can Begin Now
Creator/Author: Shelly Culbertson; Anthony C. Ross; Kobi Ruthenberg; Robert Lane; Shireen Shelleh
Object Description (Abstract): This book proposes spatial planning strategies for an independent Palestinian state based on political science and economics concepts. Based on interviews, historical plans, literature review, consultations, and site visits, it develops plans for six cities or future cities in Palestine, It argues that these projects in increments can alleviate and potentially resolve aspects of the conflict by improving the daily lives of Palestinians. The authors are American RAND researchers. Background knowledge on the Israel-Palestine conflict is ideal. 156 pages; college reading level.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: city planning; infrastructure; resource management

Title: The Politics of Maps: Cartographic Constructions of Israel/Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): This book is based in cartography and geographical science, analyzing how mapping practices shape territorial claims in Israel/Palestine. Using historical maps, institutional analysis, and cartographer interviews, it examines naming, surveying, the making of borders, including comparisons to South Africa and Ukraine. It argues that boundary-making has no absolute solution, but that understanding the complexity of the cartography is crucial to understanding the root of the conflict. Minimal background knowledge is required. The authors are professors at Cornell and Tel Aviv University. 244 pages; post-graduate reading level.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: maps; Oslo accords; sovereignty; national identity; Israel-Palestine conflict; The Naksa; The 6 Day War; 1960s

Title: An Aesthetic Occupation: The Immediacy of Architecture and the Palestine Conflict
Object Description (Abstract): This book covers the role of sacred architecture in politics in Palestine, particularly during the British Mandate era. Knowledge from the fields of architecture, political science, theology, and history form the basis for this analysis, Drawing on archival research, political theory, and analysis of discourse around holy site authenticity, it documents Palestinian, Zionist, and British attitudes towards sacred architecture in the 1920s to 1940s. It argues that monuments and political violence are fundamentally tied to each other, and thus sacred architecture is important to understanding the conflict. Background information on the British mandate era and Zionism are important. The author is a professor of Geography and Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies at Colgate University. 272 pages; upper-level undergraduate reading level.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: architecture; religion; monuments; holy sites; Zionism; Israel-Palestine conflict; British Mandate; architecture; 1920s; 1930s; 1940s; Buraq riots

Title: Shrinking Spaces, Excluded Communities, and Transformed Environments: Exploring Health and Environment among Palestinians through Eco-Social Approaches.
Creator/Author: Osama Tanous; Brian Boyd; Maysaa Nemer
Academic Discipline: public health; environmental health; medical anthropology
Object Description (Abstract): This article in public health, environmental health, and medical anthropology examines the health impacts of colonial environmental transformation in Palestine since 1948. Using public health literature, environmental data, and case examples, the authors apply ecosocial theory to the conflict to connect exclusionary spatial policies to adverse health outcomes for Palestinians. It argues that health is a structural and ecological issue shaped by the colonial engineering of space. Some familiarity with public health concepts is helpful. The authors’ national identities are unknown, but they are affiliated with the Jerusalem Quarterly journal. 2 pages; lower-undergraduate reading level.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: colonialism; health; environment; The Nakba; 1940s

Title: Sacred Landscape: The Buried History of the Holy Land since 1948
Object Description (Abstract): This book investigates the transformation of the Palestinian landscape after 1948, approaching the situation from a history, geography, and political science perspective.
