Using toponymic records, historical maps, demographic data, case studies, and the author’s personal experience, the text analyzes renaming and resignification processes used to build an Israeli national identity in the region. It argues that symbolic landscape changes were central to creating an Israeli identity, while erasing Palestinian memory tied to the landscape. Historical background knowledge on the Nakba and formation of Israel are helpful for understanding this dense text written by an Israeli political scientist. 376 pages; high-school reading level.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: The Nakba; 1940s; maps; natural landscapes; memory

Title: Reclaiming Spaces: The 50 Village Rehabilitation Project in Rural Palestine
Object Description (Abstract): This book documents community-based rehabilitation in rural Palestinian villages in Gaza and the West Bank. It has a foundation in heritage studies, anthropology, and architecture. Using images of projects, rehabilitation theory essays, and six case studies, it applies conservation and rehabilitation theory to restore these Palestinian villages. It argues that rural architectural rehabilitation can function as spatial resistance and socioeconomic revitalization. Minimal background knowledge is needed for understanding. The authors are Palestinian, with professional experience in architecture, restoration, and anthropology. 236 pages; undergraduate reading difficulty
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: architecture; decolonization; Rural Palestine; 2000s; 2010s; 50 Village Rehabilitation Project; conservation; spatial resistance

Title: Against the Jerusalem Narrative: Spatial Politics of Jaffa-Tel Aviv
Academic Discipline: urban studies; architecture; political science; geography
Object Description (Abstract): This podcast episode relates to the fields of urban studies, architecture, political science, and geography. It analyzes spatial politics and segregation in Jaffa-Tel Aviv historically and currently. Contextualizing using the history of the area and offering critique of planning and gentrification, this episode links segregation in Israel/Palestine to broader colonial and global urban processes. It argues that spatial violence operates beyond just militarized zones by way of every day urban restructuring. Some background knowledge of current gentrification in Jaffa-Tel Aviv is helpful. The podcast is hosted by a French architect, with a Palestinian architect and urbanist as the episode’s guest. 41 minutes long; low difficulty of comprehension.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: gentrification; urbicide; colonialism; spatial violence; The Naksa; The 6 Day War; 1960s; capitalism

Title: Land Use Planning in the Occupied Palestinian Territories
Object Description (Abstract): This article is focused in the fields of urban studies, political science, and geography. The author examines land use planning as a mechanism of territorial control in the West Bank and Gaza. Using planning regulations, policy analysis, demographic information, and infrastructure data, the paper applies planning and engineering concepts to analyze Israeli control strategies between the period of 1967 to its writing in 1990. It argues that land use planning restricted Palestinian development and facilitated future expropriation by Israeli powers. Knowledge of Israel/Palestine relations in the 70s and 80s is useful for context. The author is a PhD engineer and founder of a Palestinian planning institution. 18 pages; upper-level undergraduate reading level.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Occupied Territories; The Naksa; The 6 Day War; 1960s; Land use planning

Title: Exploring Palestine's Musical Heriage before 1948: An Interview with Nader Jalal
Object Description (Abstract): In this magazine interview with Nader Jalal, published by NO-NIN, he advocates for the preservation and revitalization of music history in Palestine pre-1948 in the Nawa Project, an archive of Palestinian music pre-1948. The interviewers, Shayma Nader and Miia Laine, interview Nader and utilize questions that engage with personal historical recount. The prose is clear and accessible, similar to its short length, lending well to overall accessibility to a wide audience.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: music; identity formation; belonging; archival work; interview

Title: Palestinian Music and the Potential for the Nation Branding of Palestine
Creator/Author: Zaid Bouziane; Souad Rahhal; Abeer Al Najjar
Object Description (Abstract): This article, “Palestinian Music and the Potential for the Nation Branding of Palestine,” by Zaid Bouziane, Souad Rahhar, and  Abeer Al Najjar, investigates music as a mode of creating a national brand/identity. Using qualitative analysis of media depicting Palestine, they argue that Palestinian musicians can add substantial value to the branding of Palestine. This article requires a bit of time to read, at 19 pages and moderate to hard difficulty, and a previous study context is included.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: music; identity formation; belonging; national identity

Title: Protest Song, Tradition, and the Performativity of Resistance in the Palestinian National Movement.
Object Description (Abstract): In this chapter, David A. McDonald, in Bloomington, Indiana, writes about music as resistance, the Palestinian crisis, and the prosocial aspects of Palestinian protest song. Using ethnographic analysis of first-hand accounts, he argues that music performance and political protest are prosocial behaviors that provide a medium for social action and political solidarity. At 16 pages long, this is an easier read with little background knowledge needed, only some basics about key time periods or resistance movements.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: music; identity formation; music as resistance; protest; belonging; national identity

Title: Modernity, Tradition, and Identity: The Transformation of Music-Making in Palestine, 1936 to 1948.
Object Description (Abstract): In this paper, Issa Boulos discusses music-making in Palestine from 1936 to 1948, specifically investigating radio stations and their influence on the spread of new music genres. Utilizing historiological review of archival records, he argues that music was a vital tool for Palestinians to consolidate their identity and to connect with regional trends. This is a longer and more complex paper at 30 pages in length and moderate difficulty, mostly regarding the musicological terminology.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: music; music-making; cultural music; identity formation; belonging

Title: Death-Defying Indigenous Dance:" Palest-Indian" Solidary Love.
Journal Title: The Journal of Comparative Literature and Aesthetics
Object Description (Abstract): In Joshua M. Hall’s forthcoming article, he investigates the relationality between the North American Indigenous Peoples’ struggle and the Palestinian struggle through the lens of dance and how dance can act as a bridge between cultures. Using comparative analysis of digital ethnography, he argues that dance in indigenous cultures can be used as an act of resistance and as a sign of solidarity between indigenous groups. Some background about the American Indigenous struggle and the Palestinian struggle is required for this article. At 15 pages in length, it is a medium-length reading at medium difficulty.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: Indigenous; dance; cultural dance; dance as resistance; identity formation; belonging

Title: Ramallah ravers and Haifa hipsters: gender, class, and nation in Palestinian popular culture.
Journal Title: British Journal of Middle Eastern Studies
Object Description (Abstract): In London scholar, Polly Wither’s article, she investigates how music and rave culture in Palestine act to subvert the dominant frameworks and structures. Using qualitative analysis between first-hand accounts, she argues that raves are only partially understood through Palestinian resistance to domination. Moderate background knowledge about rave culture is helpful. And at 21 pages, this is a longer read with moderate difficulty.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: modern dance; culture; dance as resistance; rave; identity formation; belonging

Title: Rerooted and Reimagined: Dance, Palestinian Women, and the Reclaimation of Urban Spaces
Journal Title: The Jerusalem Academy of Music and Dance
Object Description (Abstract): In this article, Jerusalem scholar Hodel Ophir investigates Palestinian choreographer Shaden Abu Elasal and the urban settings she uses for her performances. This highlights how urban choreography serves as a mode of deepening connection to cultural land and reimagining oppressive structures. Through first-hand accounts, interviews, and choreography investigations, Ophir argues that staging dances in locations that hold significant historical meaning deepens roots to the land and brings together an identity of space. While this paper is lengthy at 18 pages, little background knowledge is needed to understand, as most topics are explained in the paper at an intermediate level of difficulty.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: modern dance; identity formation; dance as resistance; urban choroegraphy; belonging

Title: The Three Enigmas of Palestinian Literature.
Object Description (Abstract): In the article “The Three Enigmas of Palestinian Literature” published by the Journal of Palestinian Studies, authored by Refqa Abu-Remaileh, who is based in Ar-Rayyan, Qatar, she discusses the three enigmas in Palestinian literature utilizing an analysis of Palestinian literature. She argues that the barriers for literary scholarship revolve around rethinking conventional categorizations, canonizations, and periodizations to better understand how a national literature came from a context of exile, fragmentation, and statelessness, and how processes of cultural production operate in extranational conditions. A moderate level of background is needed on the history of Palestine in this 5-page article. Although short, it is an intermediate-level article, and most concepts are explained in the article.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: literature; literary analysis; identity formation; belonging

Title: Country of Words: Palestinian Literature in the Digital Age of the Refugee
Object Description (Abstract): In the article “Country of Words: Palestinian Literature in the Digital Age of Refugee” by Refqa Abu-Remaileh, published by the Journal of Arabic Literature, she explores how to welcome the scattered and unconventional elements and nature of Palestinian literature within digital humanities. Using qualitative analysis and data analysis to investigate primary and secondary sources, she argues that opening a new scope and methodology of how we engage with Palestinian literature impacts how we understand Palestinian literature and its multifaceted and fragmented nature. Using digital humanistic analysis, we can piece together a map of Palestinian literature and similar projects. Some background knowledge is needed in regard to the Palestinian diaspora and digital humanistic work; most of the material is either explained or does not go super in-depth. The article is longer at 30 pages and is of intermediate difficulty.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: digital humanities; literature; identity formation; belonging; literary history

Title: "What Is This Palestine, Anyway?": Two Second-Generation Palestinian American Women Negotiate Roots and Routes.
Object Description (Abstract): In the article ““What is This Palestine, Anyway?”: Two Second Generation Palestinian American Women Negotiate Roots and Routes” by Mai Serhan, published by the Journal of Palestinian Studies, she argues for the necessity to narrate such experiences or risk being outwritten by various Others. To achieve this, Serhan uses comparative analysis of primary sources in literature. The article is medium length at 12 pages and is beginner-friendly, with excerpts from the memoirs and the context broken down.
Keywords/Subjects/Tags: literature; comparative literature; identity formation; diaspora; belonging

Title: Geographies of the Body: Music, Violence and Manhood in Palestine
